visualizations.title: What Makes a Physical Quantity
visualizations.description: A physical quantity always needs a number and a unit, and splits into scalars or vectors

visualizations.title: Vector Magnitude: Combining Its Components
visualizations.description: How a vector's size (magnitude) comes from its parts, showing why direction is baked into a vector

steps.explanation: A vector like velocity can be split into how much it moves sideways (v_x) and how much up/down (v_y).
The magnitude (just a number, like the scalar 'speed') comes from combining the components with the Pythagorean theorem.
The direction (angle) is the other piece that turns a plain number into a full vector.

visualizations.title: A Vector Has Direction
visualizations.description: An arrow spinning to show that a vector isn't just a size — it always points somewhere

visualizations.title: Scalar vs Vector, Side by Side
visualizations.description: A speedometer (scalar: just a number) next to a compass arrow (vector: number and direction)

visualizations.title: Constant Speed = Distance Growing Steadily
visualizations.description: At a constant scalar speed of 20 m/s, distance travelled grows in a straight line over time

visualizations.title: Number + Unit, Every Time
visualizations.description: A quick recap of physical quantities

spec.body_markdown:
## Physical Quantities

A physical quantity is anything you can measure, and it always needs two parts:

- **Number** - how much (5, 2, 60...)
- **Unit** - in what (meters, kg, km/h...)

They split into two families:

- **Scalars** - just a number: mass (10 kg), temperature (25 C), time (10 s)
- **Vectors** - a number AND a direction: velocity (10 m/s north), force (50 N upward)

'100 N' and '100 N downward' are not the same statement - direction changes what the quantity actually means.

visualizations.title: Build a Vector
visualizations.description: Drag the magnitude and angle sliders to see how a number and a direction together make a vector

visualizations.title: Scalars and Vectors: Concept Map
visualizations.description: Examples of each family of physical quantity
